forecasts.reasoning: The Artemis Accords have shown a strong and sustained growth rate, expanding from 47 signatories at the end of 2024 to 55 by mid-May 2025 (an average of ~1.6 new signatories per month). The Accords were initiated during the first Trump administration, so the 2025 administration change has likely only bolstered momentum, as evidenced by the consistent early 2025 signings. Historically, signings often occur in clusters around bilateral meetings or international space conferences (like the UN COPUOS main session in June). June has seen signings in both 2023 (India, Ecuador) and 2024 (Armenia). While signatures do not happen every single month and can be bursty, the overall high frequency of new additions (~65-75% of months see at least one signing) combined with the historical precedent of June activity suggests a high likelihood. A 72% probability accounts for the strong base rate while leaving appropriate uncertainty for the possibility of a quiet month.

The forecast horizon is extremely short—just 32 days from the May 29, 2025, cutoff to the July 1, 2025, resolution date. While Fetterman is facing controversy ('article blowback,' missing votes, questions about his health and mental fitness) and strained relationships with the Democratic party, he has repeatedly denied any intention to resign or leave the Democratic caucus. Resignations or caucus switches rarely occur over such a short timescale, especially after explicit refusals to resign, as politicians typically attempt to weather the storm first. Furthermore, he explicitly ruled out the 'Manchin or Sinema play' and stated he would make a 'bad Republican.' Given these factors, the only feasible mechanisms for a YES resolution are a sudden deterioration in his health or an immediate, overwhelming intensification of the current scandal that forces a resignation within four weeks. The base rate for such an event within a single month is extremely low. Therefore, the probability of him leaving the caucus before July 1 is estimated at 6%.
